subject: Mathematics
q: If matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} x & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & y & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & z \end{bmatrix}$, $xyz = 50$ and $8x + 4y + 3z = 30$, then $A(\mathrm{adj}\,A)$ is equal to
question: If matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} x & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & y & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & z \end{bmatrix}$, $xyz = 50$ and $8x + 4y + 3z = 30$, then $A(\mathrm{adj}\,A)$ is equal to
solution:
For any square matrix $A$, we know that

$$ A(\operatorname{adj} A)=|A|I $$

So we only need to find $\det(A)$.

Given

$$ A=\begin{bmatrix} x & 3 & 2\\ 1 & y & 4\\ 2 & 2 & z \end{bmatrix} $$

Now expand the determinant along the first row:

$$ |A|=x\begin{vmatrix} y & 4\\ 2 & z \end{vmatrix} -3\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 4\\ 2 & z \end{vmatrix} +2\begin{vmatrix} 1 & y\\ 2 & 2 \end{vmatrix} $$

$$ = x(yz-8)-3(z-8)+2(2-2y) $$

$$ = xyz-8x-3z+24+4-4y $$

$$ = xyz-(8x+4y+3z)+28 $$

Now use the given values:

$$ xyz=50,\qquad 8x+4y+3z=30 $$

So,

$$ |A|=50-30+28=48 $$

Hence,

$$ A(\operatorname{adj} A)=|A|I=48I $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{48I} $$

Option B.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $a, b, c > 0$ & $x, y, z \in \mathbb{R}$, then the determinant

$ \left| \begin{array}{ccc} (a^x + a^{-x})^2 & (a^x - a^{-x})^2 & 1 \\ (b^y + b^{-y})^2 & (b^y - b^{-y})^2 & 1 \\ (c^z + c^{-z})^2 & (c^z - c^{-z})^2 & 1 \end{array} \right| $
question:
If $a, b, c > 0$ & $x, y, z \in \mathbb{R}$, then the determinant

$ \left| \begin{array}{ccc} (a^x + a^{-x})^2 & (a^x - a^{-x})^2 & 1 \\ (b^y + b^{-y})^2 & (b^y - b^{-y})^2 & 1 \\ (c^z + c^{-z})^2 & (c^z - c^{-z})^2 & 1 \end{array} \right| $
solution:
To find the value of the given determinant, we can use a basic algebraic identity to simplify the terms inside it. Let's write down the determinant first:

$$ \Delta = \left| \begin{array}{ccc} (a^x + a^{-x})^2 & (a^x - a^{-x})^2 & 1 \\ (b^y + b^{-y})^2 & (b^y - b^{-y})^2 & 1 \\ (c^z + c^{-z})^2 & (c^z - c^{-z})^2 & 1 \end{array} \right| $$

Notice the pattern in the first and second columns. They look like $(p + q)^2$ and $(p - q)^2$. We know from basic algebra that:

$$(p + q)^2 - (p - q)^2 = 4pq$$

Let's subtract the second column from the first column. Our operation will be $C_1 \rightarrow C_1 - C_2$.

For the first element:

$$(a^x + a^{-x})^2 - (a^x - a^{-x})^2 = 4(a^x)(a^{-x}) = 4(a^0) = 4(1) = 4$$

For the second element:

$$(b^y + b^{-y})^2 - (b^y - b^{-y})^2 = 4(b^y)(b^{-y}) = 4(b^0) = 4(1) = 4$$

For the third element:

$$(c^z + c^{-z})^2 - (c^z - c^{-z})^2 = 4(c^z)(c^{-z}) = 4(c^0) = 4(1) = 4$$

After applying the column operation, all the elements in the first column become $4$. The determinant now looks like this:

$$ \Delta = \left| \begin{array}{ccc} 4 & (a^x - a^{-x})^2 & 1 \\ 4 & (b^y - b^{-y})^2 & 1 \\ 4 & (c^z - c^{-z})^2 & 1 \end{array} \right| $$

$$ \Delta = 4 \left| \begin{array}{ccc} 1 & (a^x - a^{-x})^2 & 1 \\ 1 & (b^y - b^{-y})^2 & 1 \\ 1 & (c^z - c^{-z})^2 & 1 \end{array} \right| $$

Looking at the simplified determinant, we can see that the first column ($C_1$) and the third column ($C_3$) are completely identical.

According to the properties of determinants, if any two rows or columns of a determinant are identical, the value of the determinant is zero.

$$ \Delta = 4 \times 0 = 0 $$

The given determinant evaluates to zero.

Option D is correct.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the roots of $x^3 + px^2 + q = 0$, where $q \neq 0$, then

$ \Delta = \left| \begin{matrix} \frac{1}{\alpha} & \frac{1}{\beta} & \frac{1}{\gamma} \\ \frac{1}{\beta} & \frac{1}{\gamma} & \frac{1}{\alpha} \\ \frac{1}{\gamma} & \frac{1}{\alpha} & \frac{1}{\beta} \end{matrix} \right| $

equals
question:
If $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the roots of $x^3 + px^2 + q = 0$, where $q \neq 0$, then

$ \Delta = \left| \begin{matrix} \frac{1}{\alpha} & \frac{1}{\beta} & \frac{1}{\gamma} \\ \frac{1}{\beta} & \frac{1}{\gamma} & \frac{1}{\alpha} \\ \frac{1}{\gamma} & \frac{1}{\alpha} & \frac{1}{\beta} \end{matrix} \right| $

equals
solution:
Let

$$ a=\frac{1}{\alpha},\quad b=\frac{1}{\beta},\quad c=\frac{1}{\gamma} $$

Then the determinant becomes

$$ \Delta= \begin{vmatrix} a & b & c\\ b & c & a\\ c & a & b \end{vmatrix} $$

$$ \Delta = a(cb-a^2)-b(b^2-ac)+c(ab-c^2) $$

$$ \Delta = abc-a^3-b^3+abc+abc-c^3 $$

$$ \Delta=3abc-(a^3+b^3+c^3) $$

Now, by Vietaâ€™s formula for

$$ x^3+px^2+q=0 $$

we have

$$ \alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha=0 $$

So,

$$ a+b+c = \frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}+\frac{1}{\gamma} = \frac{\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha}{\alpha\beta\gamma} =0 $$

Using the identity

$$ a^3+b^3+c^3-3abc=(a+b+c)(a^2+b^2+c^2-ab-bc-ca) $$

Since $a+b+c=0$,

$$ a^3+b^3+c^3=3abc $$

Hence,

$$ \Delta=3abc-(a^3+b^3+c^3)=0 $$

So the value of the determinant is $0$, which is not among Options A, B, or C.

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: None of these}} $$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $a - 2b + c = 1$, then the value of

$ \begin{vmatrix} x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\ x+2 & x+3 & x+b \\ x+3 & x+4 & x+c \end{vmatrix} $

is
question:
If $a - 2b + c = 1$, then the value of

$ \begin{vmatrix} x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\ x+2 & x+3 & x+b \\ x+3 & x+4 & x+c \end{vmatrix} $

is
solution:
Apply $R_2 \to R_2 - R_1$ and $R_3 \to R_3 - R_1$:

$$\begin{vmatrix} x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\ 1 & 1 & b-a \\ 2 & 2 & c-a \end{vmatrix}$$

Now apply $R_3 \to R_3 - 2R_2$:

$$\begin{vmatrix} x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\ 1 & 1 & b-a \\ 0 & 0 & c-a-2(b-a) \end{vmatrix}$$

The last entry of $R_3$ becomes $c - a - 2b + 2a = a - 2b + c$.

Since we are given $a - 2b + c = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$\begin{vmatrix} x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\ 1 & 1 & b-a \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$$

$$1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} x+1 & x+2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (x+1) - (x+2) = -1$$

So the value of the determinant is $-1$.

The correct option is C.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $x > 0$, $y > 0$, $z > 0$ be, respectively, the $2^{nd}$, $3^{rd}$, $4^{th}$ terms of a G.P and

$\Delta = \left| \begin{matrix} x^k & x^{k+1} & x^{k+2} \\ y^k & y^{k+1} & y^{k+2} \\ z^k & z^{k+1} & z^{k+2} \end{matrix} \right| = (r-1)^2 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{r^2} \right)$

(where $r$ is the common ratio) Then
question:
Let $x > 0$, $y > 0$, $z > 0$ be, respectively, the $2^{nd}$, $3^{rd}$, $4^{th}$ terms of a G.P and

$\Delta = \left| \begin{matrix} x^k & x^{k+1} & x^{k+2} \\ y^k & y^{k+1} & y^{k+2} \\ z^k & z^{k+1} & z^{k+2} \end{matrix} \right| = (r-1)^2 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{r^2} \right)$

(where $r$ is the common ratio) Then
solution:
Since $x, y, z$ are the 2nd, 3rd and 4th terms of a G.P. with common ratio $r$, we can write

$$x = ar,\quad y = ar^2,\quad z = ar^3$$

where $a$ is the first term.

Now look at the determinant. Take $x^k$, $y^k$, $z^k$ common from the three rows:

$$\Delta = x^k y^k z^k \left| \begin{matrix} 1 & x & x^2 \\ 1 & y & y^2 \\ 1 & z & z^2 \end{matrix} \right|$$

The determinant on the right is the well-known Vandermonde determinant, whose value is $(y-x)(z-x)(z-y)$.

So

$$\Delta = (xyz)^k (y-x)(z-x)(z-y)$$

Let's evaluate each factor using $x=ar,\ y=ar^2,\ z=ar^3$.

$$xyz = a^3 r^6$$

$$y - x = ar^2 - ar = ar(r-1)$$

$$z - x = ar^3 - ar = ar(r^2-1) = ar(r-1)(r+1)$$

$$z - y = ar^3 - ar^2 = ar^2(r-1)$$

Multiplying these three,

$$(y-x)(z-x)(z-y) = a^3 r^4 (r-1)^3 (r+1)$$

So

$$\Delta = (a^3 r^6)^k \cdot a^3 r^4 (r-1)^3 (r+1)$$

Now simplify the given RHS:

$$(r-1)^2\left(1 - \frac{1}{r^2}\right) = (r-1)^2 \cdot \frac{r^2-1}{r^2} = \frac{(r-1)^3(r+1)}{r^2}$$

So we need

$$(a^3 r^6)^k \cdot a^3 r^4 (r-1)^3(r+1) = \frac{(r-1)^3(r+1)}{r^2}$$

$$a^{3k+3}\, r^{6k+4} = r^{-2}$$

For this to hold for all valid $a$ and $r$, we need the power of $a$ to be zero and the power of $r$ to match:

$$3k+3 = 0 \Rightarrow k = -1$$

Check the $r$ part: $6(-1)+4 = -2$. âś“

So $k = -1$, which is option A.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $a, b, c, d > 0$; $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $(a^2 + b^2 + c^2)x^2 - 2(ab + bc + cd)x + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 \leq 0$, then

$\left|\begin{array}{lll}33 & 14 & \log a \\ 65 & 27 & \log b \\ 97 & 40 & \log c\end{array}\right|=$
question:
If $a, b, c, d > 0$; $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $(a^2 + b^2 + c^2)x^2 - 2(ab + bc + cd)x + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 \leq 0$, then

$\left|\begin{array}{lll}33 & 14 & \log a \\ 65 & 27 & \log b \\ 97 & 40 & \log c\end{array}\right|=$
solution:
We have,

$$(ax-b)^2+(bx-c)^2+(cx-d)^2 \leq 0$$

$$\Rightarrow (ax-b)^2=(bx-c)^2=(cx-d)^2=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=\frac{c}{b}=\frac{d}{c}=x$$

$$\Rightarrow b^2=ac \text{ or } 2\log b=\log a+\log c$$

Given that

$$\Delta=\begin{vmatrix} 33 & 14 & \log a\\ 65 & 27 & \log b\\ 97 & 40 & \log c \end{vmatrix}=$$

Applying $R_1 \rightarrow R_1+R_3$, we get

$$\Delta=\begin{vmatrix} 130 & 54 & \log a+\log c\\ 65 & 27 & \log b\\ 97 & 40 & \log c \end{vmatrix}$$

Applying $R_1 \rightarrow R_1-2R_2$, we get

$$\Delta=\begin{vmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0\\ 65 & 27 & \log b\\ 97 & 40 & \log c \end{vmatrix}$$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let A denote the collection of all determinants of order 3 whose entries are either 0 or 1. Let B be the subset of A containing those determinants whose value is 1, and let C be the subset of A containing those determinants whose value is $-1$. Then

STATEMENT-1 : The number of elements present in set B is the same as the number of elements present in set C.

and

STATEMENT-2 : $(B \cap C) \subseteq A$
question:
Let A denote the collection of all determinants of order 3 whose entries are either 0 or 1. Let B be the subset of A containing those determinants whose value is 1, and let C be the subset of A containing those determinants whose value is $-1$. Then

STATEMENT-1 : The number of elements present in set B is the same as the number of elements present in set C.

and

STATEMENT-2 : $(B \cap C) \subseteq A$
options: STATEMENT-1 is true, STATEMENT-2 is true and STATEMENT-2 is correct explanation for STATEMENT-1
STATEMENT-1 is true, STATEMENT-2 is true and STATEMENT-2 is not correct explanation for STATEMENT-1
solution:
Here $A$ contains all $3 \times 3$ determinants whose entries are only $0$ or $1$.

$B$ contains those determinants whose value is $1$.

$C$ contains those determinants whose value is $-1$.

To compare the number of elements in $B$ and $C$, use the property of determinants:

If we interchange any two rows of a determinant, its sign changes.

So, if a determinant has value $1$, then after interchanging two rows, its value becomes $-1$.

For example, if

$$ \Delta = 1 $$

then after interchanging two rows,

$$ \Delta' = -1 $$

Also, since the determinant value is non-zero, no two rows are identical. So interchanging two rows gives a different determinant.

This gives a one-one pairing between determinants of value $1$ and determinants of value $-1$.

Hence,

$$ n(B)=n(C) $$

So, STATEMENT-1 is true.

Now, since $B$ and $C$ are both subsets of $A$,

$$ B \cap C \subseteq A $$

So, STATEMENT-2 is also true.

But STATEMENT-2 only says that the common elements of $B$ and $C$ lie in $A$. It does not explain why $n(B)=n(C)$.

Hence, STATEMENT-2 is not the correct explanation for STATEMENT-1.

The correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option B}} $$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $Y = SX$ and $Z = tX$, all the variables being differentiable functions of $x$. If

$\left|\begin{array}{lll}X & Y & Z \\ X_1 & Y_1 & Z_1 \\ X_2 & Y_2 & Z_2\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ll}S_1 & t_1 \\ S_2 & t_2\end{array}\right|=X^n$,

where lower suffices denote the derivative with respect to $x$, then $n =$
question:
Let $Y = SX$ and $Z = tX$, all the variables being differentiable functions of $x$. If

$\left|\begin{array}{lll}X & Y & Z \\ X_1 & Y_1 & Z_1 \\ X_2 & Y_2 & Z_2\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ll}S_1 & t_1 \\ S_2 & t_2\end{array}\right|=X^n$,

where lower suffices denote the derivative with respect to $x$, then $n =$
solution:
Using $Y=SX$ and $Z=tX$, the first determinant is

$$ \begin{vmatrix} X & Y & Z\\ X_1 & Y_1 & Z_1\\ X_2 & Y_2 & Z_2 \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} X & SX & tX\\ X_1 & S_1X+SX_1 & t_1X+tX_1\\ X_2 & S_2X+2S_1X_1+SX_2 & t_2X+2t_1X_1+tX_2 \end{vmatrix}. $$

Now use column operations:

$$ C_2 \to C_2-SC_1,\qquad C_3 \to C_3-tC_1. $$

Then the determinant becomes

$$ \begin{vmatrix} X & 0 & 0\\ X_1 & XS_1 & Xt_1\\ X_2 & XS_2+2X_1S_1 & Xt_2+2X_1t_1 \end{vmatrix}. $$

Expanding along the first row,

$$ =X \begin{vmatrix} XS_1 & Xt_1\\ XS_2+2X_1S_1 & Xt_2+2X_1t_1 \end{vmatrix}. $$

So,

$$ =X\left[X^2(S_1t_2-S_2t_1)\right] $$

because the terms containing $2X_1S_1t_1$ cancel.

Hence,

$$ \begin{vmatrix} X & Y & Z\\ X_1 & Y_1 & Z_1\\ X_2 & Y_2 & Z_2 \end{vmatrix} = X^3 \begin{vmatrix} S_1 & t_1\\ S_2 & t_2 \end{vmatrix}. $$

So the power of $X$ is

$$ n=3. $$

Correct option: C.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Given

$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{1}{c} & \frac{1}{c} & -\frac{(a+b)}{c^2} \\ -\frac{(b+c)}{a^2} & \frac{1}{a} & \frac{1}{a} \\ -\frac{b(b+c)}{a^2 c} & \frac{(a+2b+c)}{ac} & -\frac{b(a+b)}{ac^2} \end{vmatrix},$

then consider the following two statements:

S1: $\Delta$ is independent of $a$

S2: $\Delta$ is independent of $b$ and $c$

Which of the following is true?
question:
Given

$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{1}{c} & \frac{1}{c} & -\frac{(a+b)}{c^2} \\ -\frac{(b+c)}{a^2} & \frac{1}{a} & \frac{1}{a} \\ -\frac{b(b+c)}{a^2 c} & \frac{(a+2b+c)}{ac} & -\frac{b(a+b)}{ac^2} \end{vmatrix},$

then consider the following two statements:

S1: $\Delta$ is independent of $a$

S2: $\Delta$ is independent of $b$ and $c$

Which of the following is true?
solution:
First, multiply the three columns by $a$, $b$, and $c$ respectively.

That means we apply $C_1 \rightarrow a C_1, C_2 \rightarrow b C_2$ and $C_3 \rightarrow c C_3$.

When columns are multiplied by $a$, $b$, and $c$, the determinant gets multiplied by $abc$. So we take out the factor $\frac{1}{abc}$.

$$ \Delta=\frac{1}{a b c}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{a}{c} & \frac{b}{c} & -\frac{a+b}{c} \\ -\frac{b+c}{a} & \frac{b}{a} & \frac{c}{a} \\ -\frac{b(b+c)}{a c} & \frac{b(a+2 b+c)}{a c} & -\frac{b(a+b)}{a c} \end{array}\right| $$

Now add the three columns inside the determinant.

For the first row,

$$\frac{a}{c}+\frac{b}{c}-\frac{a+b}{c}=0$$

For the second row,

$$-\frac{b+c}{a}+\frac{b}{a}+\frac{c}{a}=0$$

For the third row,

$$-\frac{b(b+c)}{ac}+\frac{b(a+2b+c)}{ac}-\frac{b(a+b)}{ac}=0$$

So, $C_1+C_2+C_3$ gives a zero column.

Applying $C_1 \rightarrow C_1+C_2+C_3$, one column becomes zero, so the determinant becomes $0$.

Hence,

$$\Delta=0$$

Since $\Delta$ is $0$, it does not depend on $a$, $b$, or $c$.

So both S1 and S2 are true.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $a=1+2+4+\ldots$ up to $n$ terms

$b=1+3+9+\ldots$ up to $n$ terms

and $c=1+5+25+\ldots$ up to $n$ terms.

If $f(n)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & 2 b & 4 c \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2^n & 3^n & 5^n\end{array}\right|$, then the value of $f(10)$ is
question:
If $a=1+2+4+\ldots$ up to $n$ terms

$b=1+3+9+\ldots$ up to $n$ terms

and $c=1+5+25+\ldots$ up to $n$ terms.

If $f(n)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & 2 b & 4 c \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2^n & 3^n & 5^n\end{array}\right|$, then the value of $f(10)$ is
solution:
Given,

$$ a=1+2+4+\cdots \text{ up to } n \text{ terms} $$

This is a G.P. with first term $1$ and common ratio $2$, so

$$ a=\frac{2^n-1}{2-1}=2^n-1 $$

Similarly,

$$ b=1+3+9+\cdots \text{ up to } n \text{ terms} $$

So,

$$ b=\frac{3^n-1}{3-1}=\frac{3^n-1}{2} $$

Hence,

$$ 2b=3^n-1 $$

Also,

$$ c=1+5+25+\cdots \text{ up to } n \text{ terms} $$

So,

$$ c=\frac{5^n-1}{5-1}=\frac{5^n-1}{4} $$

Hence,

$$ 4c=5^n-1 $$

Now the determinant becomes

$$ f(n)= \begin{vmatrix} 2^n-1 & 3^n-1 & 5^n-1 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2^n & 3^n & 5^n \end{vmatrix} $$

Apply the row operation

$$ R_3 \to R_3 - R_1 $$

Then the third row becomes

$$ (2^n-(2^n-1),\ 3^n-(3^n-1),\ 5^n-(5^n-1))=(1,1,1) $$

So,

$$ f(n)= \begin{vmatrix} 2^n-1 & 3^n-1 & 5^n-1 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} $$

Here the second row is twice the third row:

$$ (2,2,2)=2(1,1,1) $$

So two rows are proportional. Hence the determinant is $0$.

Thus,

$$ f(10)=0 $$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $x, y, z$ are non-zero real numbers such that $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+x & 1 & 1 \\ 1+y & 1+2 y & 1 \\ 1+z & 1+z & 1+3 z\end{array}\right|=0$, then $\left|\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{z}\right|$ is equal to

$\_\_\_\_$ .
question:
If $x, y, z$ are non-zero real numbers such that $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+x & 1 & 1 \\ 1+y & 1+2 y & 1 \\ 1+z & 1+z & 1+3 z\end{array}\right|=0$, then $\left|\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{z}\right|$ is equal to

$\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
The given determinant is

$$ \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1+x & 1 & 1 \\ 1+y & 1+2 y & 1 \\ 1+z & 1+z & 1+3 z \end{array}\right|=0 $$

We apply column operations to make the determinant simpler. Replacing one column by the difference of two columns does not change the value of the determinant.

Applying $C_1 \rightarrow C_1-C_2, C_2 \rightarrow C_2-C_3$, we get

$$ \begin{array}{rlcc} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x & 0 & 1 \\ -y & 2 y & 1 \\ 0 & -2 z & 1+3 z \end{array}\right|=0 \\ \therefore & 2 x y(1+3 z)+2 y z+2 z x=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x y+3 x y z+y z+z x=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{z}=-3 \end{array} $$

Since $x, y, z$ are non-zero, division by $xyz$ is allowed.

So,

$$ \left|\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{z}\right|=|-3|=3 $$

Hence, the value is $3$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{n \times n}, n$ is odd. Then $\operatorname{det}\left(\left(A-A^T\right)^{2009}\right)$ is equal to
question: Let $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{n \times n}, n$ is odd. Then $\operatorname{det}\left(\left(A-A^T\right)^{2009}\right)$ is equal to
solution:
Let

$$ B=A-A^T $$

Then take transpose:

$$ B^T=(A-A^T)^T=A^T-A=-\left(A-A^T\right)=-B $$

So $B$ is a skew-symmetric matrix.

Now,

$$ \det(B)=\det(B^T) $$

But since $B^T=-B$,

$$ \det(B^T)=\det(-B) $$

For an $n \times n$ matrix,

$$ \det(-B)=(-1)^n\det(B) $$

Given that $n$ is odd, we have $(-1)^n=-1$. Hence,

$$ \det(B)=-\det(B) $$

So,

$$ 2\det(B)=0 $$

which gives

$$ \det(B)=0 $$

Now,

$$ \det\left((A-A^T)^{2009}\right)=\det(B^{2009}) $$

Using $\det(B^k)=(\det B)^k$,

$$ \det(B^{2009})=(\det B)^{2009}=0^{2009}=0 $$

So,

$$ \boxed{0} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}a & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & a & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & a\end{array}\right]$, then value of $|\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} A))| \cdot\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(A^{-1}\right)\right|=a^\lambda$ then $\lambda$ is equal to
question: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}a & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & a & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & a\end{array}\right]$, then value of $|\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} A))| \cdot\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(A^{-1}\right)\right|=a^\lambda$ then $\lambda$ is equal to
solution:
Given

$$ A=\begin{bmatrix} a&0&0\\ 0&a&0\\ 0&0&a \end{bmatrix}=aI_3 $$

Since $A^{-1}$ is used, we take $a\neq 0$.

For a $3\times 3$ matrix of the form $kI_3$, its adjoint is

$$ \operatorname{adj}(kI_3)=k^2I_3 $$

So,

$$ \operatorname{adj}A=\operatorname{adj}(aI_3)=a^2I_3 $$

Again,

$$ \operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}A) =\operatorname{adj}(a^2I_3) =(a^2)^2I_3=a^4I_3 $$

And once more,

$$ \operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}A)) =\operatorname{adj}(a^4I_3) =(a^4)^2I_3=a^8I_3 $$

Now its determinant is

$$ \left|\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}A))\right| = |a^8I_3| = (a^8)^3 = a^{24} $$

Also,

$$ A^{-1}=\frac1a I_3 $$

So,

$$ \operatorname{adj}(A^{-1}) = \operatorname{adj}\left(\frac1a I_3\right) = \left(\frac1a\right)^2I_3 = a^{-2}I_3 $$

Hence,

$$ |\operatorname{adj}(A^{-1})| = |a^{-2}I_3| = (a^{-2})^3 = a^{-6} $$

Now multiply:

$$ \left|\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}A))\right| \cdot |\operatorname{adj}(A^{-1})| = a^{24}\cdot a^{-6} = a^{18} $$

Given this is equal to $a^\lambda$, we get

$$ \lambda=18 $$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{n \times n}$ square matrix such that $a_{i j}=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}1, & \text { if } i \neq j \\ 0, & \text { if } i=j\end{array}\right.$ and in the inverse of $A$ each diagonal element is equal to $\frac{k-n}{n-1}$, where $k$ is a numerical quantity, then $k$ must be equal to
question: Let $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{n \times n}$ square matrix such that $a_{i j}=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}1, & \text { if } i \neq j \\ 0, & \text { if } i=j\end{array}\right.$ and in the inverse of $A$ each diagonal element is equal to $\frac{k-n}{n-1}$, where $k$ is a numerical quantity, then $k$ must be equal to
solution:
The given matrix $A$ has $0$ on the diagonal and $1$ everywhere else.

So it can be written as

$$ A=J-I $$

where $J$ is the $n \times n$ matrix whose every entry is $1$, and $I$ is the identity matrix.

We know that for such a matrix,

$$ A^{-1}=-I+\frac{1}{n-1}J $$

Now, the diagonal entries of $A^{-1}$ will come from the diagonal entries of both matrices.

On the diagonal:

  • diagonal entry of $-I$ is $-1$

  • diagonal entry of $\frac{1}{n-1}J$ is $\frac{1}{n-1}$

So each diagonal element of $A^{-1}$ is

$$ -1+\frac{1}{n-1} $$

$$ =\frac{-(n-1)+1}{n-1} $$

$$ =\frac{2-n}{n-1} $$

But it is given that each diagonal element is

$$ \frac{k-n}{n-1} $$

So,

$$ \frac{k-n}{n-1}=\frac{2-n}{n-1} $$

Hence,

$$ k-n=2-n $$

$$ k=2 $$

So the value of $k$ is

$$ \boxed{2} $$
